title: SEGMENT 14 — Casgevy and Lyfgenia — the first CRISPR approvals

lines.text: SEGMENT 14 — Casgevy and Lyfgenia — the first CRISPR approvals
December 8, 2023.
Two products approved on the same day for the same disease — sickle cell disease — using two different mechanisms.
Casgevy, the CRISPR-based therapy from Vertex Pharmaceuticals and CRISPR Therapeutics.
Lyfgenia, the lentiviral-based therapy from Bluebird Bio.
Both for sickle cell disease in patients twelve years and older with recurrent vaso-occlusive crises.
The simultaneous approval was, in retrospect, a perfectly chosen moment.

Sickle cell disease had been the moral and scientific cause célèbre of gene therapy for years — a disease that disproportionately affects Black patients in the United States and around the world, a disease for which the gene therapy approach is mechanistically compelling, a disease whose treatment had been promised by gene therapy advocates for decades.
The simultaneous approval of two different gene therapies for the same disease on the same day was a statement about the maturity of the field.
Let me focus on Casgevy first, because it carries the additional historical weight of being the first CRISPR-based therapy approved anywhere in the world.
Casgevy works ex vivo.
Stem cells are collected from the patient's bone marrow.
The cells are edited in a laboratory using CRISPR-Cas9 to disrupt a regulatory element in the BCL11A gene, which is a transcriptional repressor of fetal hemoglobin.
With BCL11A disrupted, the patient's red blood cell precursors start producing fetal hemoglobin instead of the defective adult hemoglobin that causes sickling.
The edited cells are then re-infused into the patient after a course of conditioning chemotherapy that wipes out their original bone marrow.
The edited cells engraft and become the new source of red blood cells, producing fetal hemoglobin that does not sickle.
The regulatory significance of Casgevy is enormous.
It is the first time CRISPR — a technology that was first described in its therapeutic form barely over a decade earlier, in the 2012 Doudna-Charpentier paper — has been approved as a drug.
Every future CRISPR program now has a regulatory precedent to work from.
The agency has demonstrated that it is willing to approve a gene-edited cell therapy.
The conversation about CRISPR therapeutics has shifted, definitively, from "could this be a drug?
" to "what should the next CRISPR drug be?
The clinical data for Casgevy was striking.
In the pivotal trial, the vast majority of treated patients — somewhere around 90 percent — experienced no vaso-occlusive crises during the twelve-month follow-up window.
Treated patients showed dramatic increases in fetal hemoglobin levels, dramatic reductions in indicators of hemolysis, and qualitative improvements in their quality of life.
The safety profile was dominated by the conditioning chemotherapy — which is itself an aggressive and potentially toxic treatment — rather than by the CRISPR editing per se.
Lyfgenia, approved the same day, takes a different approach.
Instead of CRISPR editing, Lyfgenia uses a lentiviral vector to add a healthy copy of a modified beta-globin gene to the patient's stem cells.
The cells are then re-infused.
The added beta-globin produces a hemoglobin variant that disrupts sickling.
The end result is similar to Casgevy — patients have reduced or eliminated vaso-occlusive crises — but the mechanism is gene addition rather than gene editing.
Let me give you the analogy I want you to hold.
Casgevy was the first CRISPR drug — sickle cell plus beta-thalassemia.
The regulatory template for future CRISPR therapeutics.

Every CRISPR program — for cardiovascular disease, for cancer, for neurological disorders, for liver diseases, for everything that is in the development pipeline today — looks back at the Casgevy regulatory file as the template.

The agency's expectations for CRISPR-specific data — characterization of editing efficiency, characterization of off-target editing, long-term genomic stability of edited cells, durability of clinical effect — were laid down in the Casgevy review.
Future programs build on that foundation.
The fact that two different gene therapies were approved for the same disease on the same day also illustrates something important about the regulatory landscape: the FDA does not pick winners.
It evaluates each application on its own merits.
If two products meet the standard, both get approved.
The market then decides — through pricing, through clinical outcomes, through patient and physician preference — which one becomes the dominant treatment for a given disease.
This is, in some sense, the system working as designed.
The regulator's job is gating, not selection.
